research_areas.title: Language
research_areas.desc: Expanding NLP capabilities to handle Indigenous linguistic structures and language densities.

positive_sentences.text: This project contributes strongly to AbInt’s Transformation Statement through its relational, Indigenous-centred approach to AI research grounded in Blackfoot knowledge, Elder guidance, and land-based dialogue.

By supporting artist residencies, community conversations, and the creation of digital and performative works, the project shifts AI inquiry away from purely technical development toward culturally grounded, community-led processes.

The project is well positioned to advance into the Imaginaries phase by further developing its dialogue-based and land-based methodologies as frameworks for imagining Indigenous-centred approaches to AI, particularly through language, storytelling, and relational understandings of knowledge and memory.
Risk appears relatively low, as the project is grounded in research-creation, dialogue, and community-led processes rather than high-risk technical deployment.

The risk of not pursuing the project would be significant, as it supports the relational and community-driven foundations necessary for Indigenous-centred AI, while also building capacity through residencies, mentorship, and shared learning across the AbInt network.

concern_sentences.text: This project shows a limited contribution to the Transformation Statement, as it centers collaboration and community engagement but does not substantially reshape how AI is developed or governed.
I do not see strong potential for this project to advance into the Imaginaries or Intelligences phases in its current form.

A risk is that the project remains primarily focused on curriculum development without clearly extending into technical, infrastructural, or network-level outcomes, which could limit its relevance to AbInt’s broader goals around AI and shared research methods.
Hard to asses since so late getting started.
I am not entirely certain how this aligns with the AbINT Transformations statement.

research_areas.title: Storytelling
research_areas.desc: Drawing on Indigenous storytelling and oral traditions to develop richer machine understandings.

positive_sentences.text: The work demonstrates capacity building, student training, and public knowledge mobilization through exhibition and artist talks, and shows an effort to situate AI development within community relationships and storytelling practices.
Risk appears relatively low, as the project is exploratory and limited in scope, with most activities focused on mentorship, learning, and early-stage development rather than complex AI deployment.
As the project develops its planned AI components, it could connect with other PODs exploring language models, community-based data practices, or culturally grounded interface design.

The strong mentorship structure and focus on learning also make this a good candidate for collaboration through workshops, shared development sessions, or student exchanges, which could help expand the work beyond its current scope while contributing to broader network capacity building.

This project contributes strongly to AbInt’s Transformation Statement by demonstrating how Indigenous knowledge systems, particularly Blackfoot metaphysics and teachings, can inform the creation of sovereign digital environments that challenge Western assumptions about space, time, and intelligence.

concern_sentences.text: I am not entirely certain how this aligns with the AbINT Transformations statement.
I do not see a strong potential for advancement here.
As described, the project feels fairly contained in scope, and it is not clear what new development would take place beyond what has already been completed.

While the work itself seems thoughtful and well situated in its specific context, the proposal does not clearly indicate how it would move forward in a meaningful way, or what new directions, outcomes, or research questions would emerge through this next phase.

In this case, the project seems to have been led primarily by a single non-Indigenous member, and it may have been stronger if the work had been co-led with an Indigenous POD member rather than centered on one individual.

research_areas.title: Environmental Stewardship
research_areas.desc: Traditional land management and stewardship practices informing AI-driven restoration systems.

concern_sentences.text: This project is challenging, because so much of what was reported is not tied to the CfP project.

research_areas.title: Socio-Neuro AI
research_areas.desc: Using IK diversity to model how humans draw on socio-cultural context to learn and decide.

positive_sentences.text: Its emphasis on collaboration with Lakota knowledge holders and on grounding technical work in community interpretation reflects the program’s goal of shifting AI research toward relational and culturally informed approaches.

The project fits naturally within the Imaginaries phase through its speculative exploration of symbolic language, dreaming, and human–AI interaction, and could contribute to that phase by further developing its conceptual and methodological framework for culturally grounded AI design.

This project contributes strongly to AbInt’s Transformation Statement by demonstrating how Indigenous knowledge systems, particularly Blackfoot metaphysics and teachings, can inform the creation of sovereign digital environments that challenge Western assumptions about space, time, and intelligence.

Through the development of an interactive Unity-based world, the work explores alternative ways of understanding relationality, energy, and place within digital media, while grounding the process in mentorship, community learning, and POD-based practices.

The project has strong potential to advance into the Imaginaries phase through further development of its conceptual framework around Indigenous metaphysics, relational space, and alternative understandings of time, energy, and environment within digital worlds.

concern_sentences.text: One risk is that the project relies on highly experimental methods combining neuroscience, symbolic analysis, and AI, which may make outcomes difficult to realize within the project timeframe or limit their usefulness to the wider network.

Although the project does not focus on core AI model development, it advances the transformation goals by building Indigenous-centred approaches to virtual space, storytelling, and digital world-making that can inform future AI and interactive system design within the network.

Risk appears relatively low, as the project is primarily focused on creative development, learning, and community-informed digital world-building rather than high-risk technical or data-driven AI systems.

projects.title: Abundant Memory, Relational Intelligence: Dialogue-Based Methodologies for Indigenous AI Project Design

positives.text: This project contributes strongly to AbInt’s Transformation Statement through its relational, Indigenous-centred approach to AI research grounded in Blackfoot knowledge, Elder guidance, and land-based dialogue.

By supporting artist residencies, community conversations, and the creation of digital and performative works, the project shifts AI inquiry away from purely technical development toward culturally grounded, community-led processes.

The project is well positioned to advance into the Imaginaries phase by further developing its dialogue-based and land-based methodologies as frameworks for imagining Indigenous-centred approaches to AI, particularly through language, storytelling, and relational understandings of knowledge and memory.
Risk appears relatively low, as the project is grounded in research-creation, dialogue, and community-led processes rather than high-risk technical deployment.

projects.title: A Dish with One Spoon Towards “Generous AI” Invention and Collaboration

positives.text: This project contributes strongly to AbInt’s Transformation Statement by actively reworking AI systems through Indigenous governance, methodologies, and relational ethics grounded in the Dish With One Spoon covenant.

Through experiments with adapting LLMs and SLMs, developing community-guided data practices, and embedding storytelling, ceremony, and collective decision-making into technical workflows, the project shifts AI development away from extractive models toward reciprocal, community-led approaches.

Its combination of technical prototyping, governance design, and capacity building through workshops, student leadership, and partnerships supports AbInt’s goal of transforming AI by aligning computation with Indigenous knowledge systems, sovereignty, and shared responsibility.

This research is well positioned to advance into the Imaginaries phase by further developing its governance frameworks, storytelling logics, and treaty-based ethics as foundations for alternative approaches to AI design.

This project contributes to AbInt’s Transformation Statement through its focus on community-led language work, collaborative curriculum development, and attention to data sovereignty in the context of language revitalization.
The work demonstrates a relational approach to knowledge creation, with teachers and community members directly shaping the learning materials and guiding how language data are generated and used.
Risk appears relatively low, as the project focuses primarily on curriculum development, collaboration with language teachers, and exploratory planning rather than complex technical implementation.

It could serve as a point of dialogue with projects focused on Indigenous data sovereignty, relational methodologies, and community-led infrastructure, especially where there is interest in language, pedagogy, or small-data approaches.

concerns.text: This project shows a limited contribution to the Transformation Statement, as it centers collaboration and community engagement but does not substantially reshape how AI is developed or governed.
I do not see strong potential for this project to advance into the Imaginaries or Intelligences phases in its current form.

positives.text: towards relational and accountable approaches co-developed with those communities.

recommendations.text: The project also aligns well with network activities focused on relational AI, digital territories, and culturally grounded interface design, and could benefit from further collaboration through shared builds, workshops, and cross-POD development sessions that expand the work beyond its original context.

Continued collaboration within the network, along with clearer pathways for sharing builds, workflows, and community-informed design methods, would help the project move from a single environment toward a more scalable and relational digital infrastructure aligned with the goals of these later phases.

For the Indigenous Methodologies book portion of their research, "The next phase of the project will largely focus on the digital component of the book, making a compelling and useful resource and methods toolbox repository for Indigenous AI scholars and practitioners." It is my recommendation that we have an website building intensive for all our related projects, and focus a lot of our RA power over a month period to getting websites up for all these amazing projects, especially one like this.

In the Intelligences phase, advancement would likely involve deepening the technical side of the work, including more fully developed SLM/LLM adaptations, community-controlled data repositories, and locally governed infrastructure, while maintaining the collective, ceremony-informed, and advisory-guided processes that shape the project.

To advance into the Intelligences phase, the work would likely need to move beyond curriculum development toward more clearly defined technical experimentation, such as prototyping language-learning tools, working with small language models, or collaborating with other projects building shared infrastructure for language data.

Greater attention to reproducibility, clearer integration with existing AbInt infrastructure efforts, and more explicit plans for how the neuro-AI and symbolic modeling components could support other Pods would help the research extend beyond a single case study into something that can inform later-phase network development.

There is also a risk of extractive dynamics, where language knowledge is structured for technical use without sufficient community control or benefit, which could be mitigated through direct evaluation from participating Indigenous community members and clearer documentation of agreements, protocols, and outcomes.

One potential risk is that the work is closely tied to a specific community context and small working group, which may limit how easily the outcomes extend beyond the immediate collaborators; this can be mitigated through clearer documentation of methods and continued dialogue with other projects in the network.

Given the goals of Abundant Intelligences, it would be helpful to see more clearly how Indigenous members were involved in shaping the direction of the work, and how responsibility, authorship, and decision-making were shared, rather than the project being led primarily by one non-Indigenous collaborator.

One potential risk is that the work depends on ongoing relationships with Elders, students, and community partners, so continuity and care are important to maintain as the project develops; this is already being addressed through advisory guidance, mentorship structures, and collective decision-making.

A potential risk is that, as a primarily student-led initiative, the work may lack continuity or long-term impact without ongoing mentorship and stronger integration with the POD or broader network; this can be mitigated through continued guidance, collaboration, and clearer next-stage planning.

In the Intelligences phase, the work could deepen by moving some of these concepts into more concrete systems, such as language learning tools, local models, or interactive digital environments, while continuing to be guided by Elder advisory processes and community priorities.

An exciting aspect of this project that needs to be supported separately is the development of a "deep-learning framework that simultaneously learns a time varying sensor footprint and a pixel level model of carbon flux applicable to biomes measured by the AmeriFlux network.

It may be helpful to clarify the extent of the community engagement described here, and to more explicitly articulate how these relationships and discussions contributed to the development of the project and how they relate to the wider objectives of Abundant Intelligences.

This could be mitigated by more explicitly linking the pedagogical work to future language technologies, tool development, or collaboration with projects building data or compute infrastructure, and by documenting processes so they can be reused across the network.

If that is the case, it would be helpful to clarify what phase the project is actually in, and whether the intention here is documentation, reflection, or further development, rather than new work that would unfold as part of the Transformations activities.

The project could move into the Imaginaries phase by further developing its community-driven pedagogical approach as a basis for thinking about how language technologies should be designed in ways that follow local knowledge systems and teaching practices.

To advance into the Intelligences phase, the work would need to move from exploratory prototypes toward more clearly defined technical outputs, such as stable platforms, documented modeling methods, or tools that can be tested or adapted by other projects.

In the Intelligences phase, advancement would likely require more concrete technical development, such as prototyping language or learning tools, along with clearer articulation of governance, data stewardship, and collaboration with other AbInt projects.

Greater documentation of methods, clearer connections to other network activities, and a more defined technical pathway would help the work extend from its current, locally focused stage into forms that can contribute more directly to the later phases.

This is one of the most connected projects because of the depth of its engagement through chapters, we should invite each chapter to present on their writing at a filmed, interview each chapter writer for a video, and host a celebration of its launch.

Stronger documentation, clearer technical goals, and closer integration with other AbInt projects would help the research extend from its current localized focus into forms that contribute more directly to later-phase network development.

In the Intelligences phase, the work could deepen by adding more interactive or adaptive systems, integrating language, narrative, or agent-based behaviours, and connecting the virtual environment to other AbInt projects or datasets.

Stronger collaboration would likely require clearer alignment with existing technical initiatives in the network and more explicit plans for how the project’s models or platforms could be adapted or used by other teams.

This is a starting point toward fine scale monitoring of carbon fluxes on Indigenous Lands beyond Coquille lands." This should be proposed in another project and linked with projects using similar technology.

Imaginaries: deepen through use the book as background reading to prep for the FIIAI Workshops; review book and extract out concrete IIAI that might be found; deepen and support by doing a second volume.

Builds lasting capacity among researchers, learners, and communities Unfortunately have pivoted away from building a technical prototype (to a proof-of-concept for protocol-driven information access).
Stronger connection to the network would likely require more intentional sharing of methods and clearer alignment with projects developing technical frameworks that could support or extend this work.
In the Imaginaries phase, the work could deepen its conceptual framework by further developing the role of community knowledge, narrative, and language as foundations for alternative AI design.
The work already proposes new conceptual models—such as Dish With One Spoon as a protocol for data sharing and reciprocity—that could be expanded into shared frameworks across the network.
Any collaboration would need to begin with transparency and alignment on ethics, governance, and community accountability before deeper integration across the network.
I think all the Hawaiian language projects could be brought together, and a consortium made with the other language projects (Maori, Niitsitapi, maybe Haudenosaunee)
Other: Imaginaries: deepen with case study of how she is consulting with her community to help other communities imagine similar engagements.

All Pods should be sent copies, and encourage local discussion sessions about it.
Book: Hemi as part of the KMB portfolio should develop a strategy to share it.

questions.text: The project carries high risk due to unclear ethical grounding and accountability, especially given the shift in partner communities and the absence of documented consent, governance agreements, or evidence of how FLAIR engages with Indigenous data sovereignty in practice.
It is somewhat unclear how these interactions extend beyond those internal conversations or how they connect more directly to the broader Transformations framework.
As described, the project feels fairly contained in scope, and it is not clear what new development would take place beyond what has already been completed.
I'd be curious to get an assessment from an Indigenous community member listed in this proposal to give feedback on how the researcher has done.

actions.text: Cordes' team proposes the following as research advancements possible in the next phase: For their local community engagement they plan to "strengthen community engagement through our connections with the Oregon Water Futures Collaborative and other water advocates across the state." In a related field, Ashely has been developing "Intro to Indigenous AI" curricula for years now, and seeks to distrubte this to "Tribal Colleges and Universities and HBCUs".
As the work involves language, cultural knowledge, and digital systems, continued attention to data governance and community consent will remain important as new tools are explored.
Because the work involves Indigenous knowledge and data, maintaining strong community guidance and careful stewardship will remain essential as technical development advances.
As the project considers future AI language tools, maintaining strong community control over data and decision-making will remain important.
This book will generate lasting capacity by publishing our early research into this field.

observations.text: Its emphasis on collaboration with community partners, development of culturally grounded curriculum materials, and experimentation with technology as a supportive rather than dominant framework aligns with the program’s goal of shifting AI research toward relational, ethical, and locally defined practices.

The project is well positioned to advance into the Imaginaries phase by further developing its dialogue-based and land-based methodologies as frameworks for imagining Indigenous-centred approaches to AI, particularly through language, storytelling, and relational understandings of knowledge and memory.

This project contributes strongly to AbInt’s Transformation Statement by demonstrating how Indigenous knowledge systems, particularly Blackfoot metaphysics and teachings, can inform the creation of sovereign digital environments that challenge Western assumptions about space, time, and intelligence.

Its focus on relational methodologies and Elder-guided processes also positions it well to contribute to network-wide conversations about ethics, data sovereignty, and Indigenous-centred design, making it a useful hub for collaboration as other projects move further into technical development.

Through experiments with adapting LLMs and SLMs, developing community-guided data practices, and embedding storytelling, ceremony, and collective decision-making into technical workflows, the project shifts AI development away from extractive models toward reciprocal, community-led approaches.

The strong mentorship structure and focus on learning also make this a good candidate for collaboration through workshops, shared development sessions, or student exchanges, which could help expand the work beyond its current scope while contributing to broader network capacity building.

The project fits naturally within the Imaginaries phase through its speculative exploration of symbolic language, dreaming, and human–AI interaction, and could contribute to that phase by further developing its conceptual and methodological framework for culturally grounded AI design.

Its combination of technical prototyping, governance design, and capacity building through workshops, student leadership, and partnerships supports AbInt’s goal of transforming AI by aligning computation with Indigenous knowledge systems, sovereignty, and shared responsibility.

The risk of not pursuing the project would be the loss of a strong example of Indigenous-centred digital environment design, which contributes to AbInt’s goals by expanding how virtual space, storytelling, and relational worldviews can shape future AI and interactive systems.

The risk of not pursuing the project would be significant, as it supports the relational and community-driven foundations necessary for Indigenous-centred AI, while also building capacity through residencies, mentorship, and shared learning across the AbInt network.
